edges.description: Reduced alpha-globin production leads to excess unpaired beta-globin chains that form unstable HbH tetramers (beta4).
edges.target: Beta-Globin Chain Excess and Hemoglobin H Formation

edges.description: HbH inclusion bodies cause oxidative membrane damage and shortened red cell survival.
edges.source: Beta-Globin Chain Excess and Hemoglobin H Formation

edges.description: Iron released from hemolyzed red cells contributes to iron loading.
Ineffective erythropoiesis suppresses hepcidin, increasing intestinal iron absorption.

nodes.description: In the absence of sufficient alpha-globin chains, excess beta-globin chains form unstable tetramers called hemoglobin H (beta4). HbH precipitates as inclusion bodies in mature red cells, causing membrane damage and premature destruction. In the most severe form (Hb Bart syndrome), excess gamma-globin chains form hemoglobin Bart (gamma4), which has extremely high oxygen affinity and cannot deliver oxygen to tissues.
nodes.id: Beta-Globin Chain Excess and Hemoglobin H Formation

evidence.snippet: varying degrees of ineffective erythropoiesis, decreased red blood cell (RBC) survival, chronic hemolytic anemia, and subsequent comorbidities

nodes.description: Peripheral destruction of red blood cells containing HbH inclusions. In HbH disease, hemolysis is typically chronic and compensated but may become acutely exacerbated by infections, oxidant drugs, or pregnancy. In Hb Bart syndrome, the anemia is severe and incompatible with extrauterine life without intervention.

evidence.snippet: may develop gallstones and experience acute episodes of hemolysis in response to infections or exposure to oxidant drugs

nodes.description: Deletions or point mutations in HBA1 and/or HBA2 genes reduce or abolish alpha-globin chain production. Large deletions are the most common cause, removing one or both alpha-globin genes on chromosome 16p13.3. Non-deletional mutations (point mutations, small insertions/deletions) tend to produce more severe phenotypes for the same number of affected alleles.

evidence.snippet: characterized by decreased synthesis of α-globin chains that results in an imbalance of α and β globin
biallelic pathogenic variants in both HBA1 and HBA2 that result in deletion or inactivation of all four
biallelic pathogenic variants in both HBA1 and HBA2 that result in deletion or inactivation of all four
biallelic pathogenic variants in HBA1 and HBA2 that result in deletion or inactivation of three

nodes.description: Expanded but ineffective erythroid compartment in bone marrow. Erythroid precursors are damaged by precipitated globin chains, leading to intramedullary apoptosis. More prominent in non-deletional HbH disease and Hb Bart syndrome.

evidence.snippet: disease modifying agents that target ineffective erythropoiesis and chronic hemolytic anemia

nodes.description: Increased serum ferritin and tissue iron deposition, particularly in transfusion-dependent patients or those with non-deletional HbH disease.
